result:
The first NIM inference run on the Spark answered in **8.9 seconds at 24.8 tokens/second** (mean 24.84 tok/s across four rounds, with a **52 ms time-to-first-token** on the streaming round) — `nim-first-inference-dgx-spark`.

That number is **latency-bound, not throughput-bound**, in the single-user sense the article tests. Re-running the benchmark at `NIM_GPU_MEM_FRACTION=0.8` vs the default `0.5` produced zero difference (24.64 vs 24.84 tok/s — within noise). The article's takeaway: *"single-request latency on an 8B FP8 model is not memory-bound. Raising NIM_GPU_MEM_FRACTION only buys headroom for concurrent requests — more KV-cache means more requests fit simultaneously — not faster answers for one caller."* So the bottleneck for one caller is per-token decode latency; throughput headroom (KV-cache / concurrency) only matters once you stack callers.
